key_points: The emergence of new dynasties often began with 'Samantas' asserting independence and performing rituals like 'Hiranya-garbha' to claim Kshatriya status.
Revenue collection was organized from producers (peasants, cattle-keepers, artisans) who were often compelled to surrender part of their produce as 'rent' to the lords.
Prashastis were composed by learned Brahmanas to depict kings as valiant, victorious warriors, though their historical accuracy is often debated by scholars.
The 'Tripartite Struggle' was a centuries-long conflict between the Gurjara-Pratihara, Rashtrakuta, and Pala dynasties for control over the strategic city of Kanauj.
Chola administration was highly organized, centered around 'Ur' (settlements of peasants) and 'Nadu' (groups of villages), which performed administrative and judicial functions.
examples: Kadamba Mayurasharman: A Brahmana who gave up his traditional profession and took to arms to establish a kingdom in Karnataka.
Kalhana: A 12th-century author who wrote 'Rajatarangini' (a history of the kings of Kashmir) using inscriptions, documents, and eyewitness accounts.
Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni: He utilized the wealth from Somnath to create a splendid capital city in Ghazni and commissioned Al-Biruni to write 'Kitab-al-Hind'.
Chola Bronze Statues: Noted globally for their craftsmanship; while most were of deities, some depicted devotees as well.
formulas: Political Authority = Military Might + Ritual Legitimacy (Brahmadeya/Prashastis)
Chola Governance Structure = Ur (Village) ightarrow Nadu (District) ightarrow Mandalam (Province)
exam_tips: Distinguish between 'Brahmadeya' (land gifted to Brahmanas) and 'Vellanvagai' (land of non-Brahmana peasant proprietors).
Understand that the 'Sabha' was an assembly of prominent Brahmana landholders that looked after temples, irrigation, and roads.
Note that the Chola navy was so powerful that they expanded their influence into Southeast Asia and across the Bay of Bengal.
Remember that Rajaraja I and his son Rajendra I were the most powerful Chola rulers who centralized the administration.
